data.title: Pending Home Sales Unexpectedly Fall 0.8% In January
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The National Association of Realtors (NAR) pending home sales index unexpectedly fell in January, dropping -0.8% to 70.9, its lowest level in series history. This was significantly worse than the expected 1.4% growth and marks a -0.4% fall from one year ago.

“Improving affordability conditions have yet to induce more buying activity,” said NAR Chief Economist Dr. Lawrence Yun. “With mortgage rates nearing 6%, an additional 5.5 million households that could not qualify for a mortgage one year ago would qualify at today’s lower rates. Most newly qualifying households do not act immediately, but based on past experience, about 10% could enter the market—potentially adding roughly 550,000 new homebuyers this year compared with last year.”

“Unless housing supply increases, these additional potential buyers becoming active in the market could simply push up home prices. This will put increasing pressure on affordability, which is why it is critical to increase supply by building more homes. Fortunately, the House of Representatives recently passed the Housing for the 21st Century Act with strong bipartisan support, an important signal that addressing the nation’s housing shortage remains a shared priority. The legislation is a meaningful step toward expanding housing supply and removing barriers that make it harder for Americans to achieve homeownership,” Yun added.

Read more

Pending Home Sales Background

The pending home sales index (PHSI) was created by the National Association of Realtors to track home sales where the contract is signed, but the transaction has not yet closed. An index of 100 is equal to the level of contract activity in 2001. The PHSI is a leading economic indicator of future existing home sales.

The chart below gives us a snapshot of the index since 2001, the first year data was analyzed.

Over this time frame, the US population has grown by 20.9%. For a better look at the underlying trend, here is an overlay with the nominal index and the population-adjusted variant. The focus is pending home sales growth since 2001.

The above chart shows the percent off turn-of-the-century values. The index for the most recent month is currently 45% below its all-time high from August 2020. The population-adjusted index is 52% off its high from April 2005.

Pending Home Sales vs. Existing Home Sales

Pending home sales measures the change in the number of homes under contract to be sold but still awaiting the closing transaction. Whereas existing home sales measure the number of home sales by mortgage closings.

The NAR explained that "because a home goes under contract a month or two before it is sold, the Pending Home Sales Index generally leads Existing-Home Sales by a month or two." The pending home sales index is considered a more relevant and accurate measure of the real estate market.

Here is a growth overlay of the two series. The general correlation, as expected, is close. A close look at the numbers supports the NAR's assessment that their pending sales series is a leading index. Since 2001, pending home sales have fallen 30.1%, while existing home sales have fallen 23.3%.

The 30-Year Fixed Mortgage Rate

According to Freddie Mac, the average 30-year fixed-rate mortgage in January 2026 was 6.10%, the lowest level in over a year. Here is a look back at the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage weekly average, beginning in April 1971.

ETFs associated with residential real-estate include iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Great Astera Labs Reset
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Although the 30% reset in Astera Labs, Inc. (ALAB) since my last coverage, driven by near-term margin pressure and sequentially slowing growth rates, the investment thesis has not been broken. The bullish thesis still stands, as Astera is structurally embedded in the hyperscaler AI architecture and has Scorpio X as a high ASP scale-up anchor to drive content growth per rack. In addition, the AWS warrant is a long-term indicator of alignment and revenue visibility.

Q4 Performance: Strong But Growth Momentum Slowed

I see a company where sales are still growing at a rapid pace but at a pace that appears to be slowing. Sales have more than doubled for the year to date, free cash flow margins are strong, and the company ends the year with a net cash position and zero debt. The foundation remains strong. What’s changed isn’t the financial foundation. What’s changed is the pace. There has also been a step-down in the rate of operating margins. There has been a reduction in GAAP operating margins, which have declined from the mid-20% range to below 20%. There has also been a step-down in adjusted operating margins.

The company is not seeing weakness in demand. It is seeing an increase in the amount of reinvestment in scale-up fabrics, optical connectivity, and next-generation switching. The company has made investments of $93.8 million in research and development in the fourth quarter of the year. This is nearly 35% of the revenue. This is likely to go higher. The operating expenses are increasing more rapidly than the revenue in the short term. This is because the company is investing in scale-up fabrics, optics, and next-generation switching. The market is discounting the optics. However, the financials are very strong.

The Protocol Debate: Noise vs. Structure

I see a debate between UALink and Ethernet Scale-Up Networking. The assumption is that there is a winner-takes-all situation. This is not the case in the infrastructure business. There are winners and losers. The losers have a very painful ride. Why is PCIe better for low-latency memory-centric communications in the data center? UALink is optimized for the same.

What about scale-out networking? Ethernet is the obvious choice. What about the latency differences? UALink is in the hundreds of nanoseconds. Ethernet scale-out networking is in the microseconds. I view Astera's strategy as very pragmatic. Astera is participating in PCIe, CXL memory expansion, UALink, and increasingly NVLink Fusion. Astera is not betting on the protocol. Astera is betting on the architecture.

The more important question is not who succeeds. The more important question is that we recognize the emergence of cluster density. As ALAB increases the number of GPUs that communicate in the scale-up fabric, it will need more switching, retimer, signal conditioning, and memory connectivity. The increase in content per rack is an absolute need that is independent of architecture. That is where the future opportunity resides.

Scorpio X: The Architectural Lever

Scorpio X is not only a new product cycle; it is a step up in Astera's business model. Scorpio X is a scale-up switching solution, unlike Scorpio P, which is a scale-out switching solution. Scale-up switching has a higher ASP and dollar content in the accelerators. Astera has framed scale-up switching as a multibillion-dollar business opportunity in the latter half of the decade. If Scorpio P and X, in combination, are greater than or equal to 50% of the revenue the next year, Scorpio X will be the growth driver of the business.

Scorpio X will be the anchor socket, which will increase the attach rate and drive the sales of retimers, memory controllers, and other connectivity products. The key to the execution of Scorpio X is the qualification timing. The initial sales will be low, and the sales will increase in 2027. If the hyperscaler ramps line up with Scorpio X, the mix will shift to higher-margin fabric switching. If the qualification timing slips, the risk of revenue concentration will increase. Scorpio X will be the upside and the risk.

The key to the execution of Scorpio X is the qualification timing. The initial sales will be low, and the sales will increase in 2027. If the hyperscaler ramps line up with Scorpio X, the mix will shift to higher-margin fabric switching.

If the qualification timing slips, the risk of revenue concentration will increase. Scorpio X will be the upside and the risk. The risk here is the timing mismatch. If the rate at which the revenues increase from Scorpio X and its adjacent scale-up products is not greater than the increase in R&D intensity, the pressure on the margins will continue.

The opportunity here is to benefit from the operating leverage that will be available once the scale-up products go into volume production, at which time the additional revenues will have high margins. Semiconductor products tend to have very sharp inflection points in their margins once the fixed investment phase of the investment curve stabilizes.

Valuation Reset After the Pullback

The valuation of the company after the pullback to the $129 price level has now reset to a much lower valuation but remains at a premium to the peer group average. The forward P/S valuation compression to 15.6x for 2026 and 12x for 2027 revenues is a sharp compression from the previous valuation levels that operated above 20x forward P/S. On the forward P/E valuation metric, the current valuation operates at 50x 2026 EPS and 35x 2027 EPS, which are high but not at the same valuation extremes as before the pullback.

It should be noted that the current revenue growth estimates are maintained at a growth rate close to 58% in 2026 and ~38% in 2027, reaching ~$2.35 billion in 2028 before normalizing. This warrants a premium valuation but not perfection pricing as before the pullback.

The current price trading at current levels means that valuation is not as extended as before but remains at a premium valuation based on the scale-up adoption and Scorpio-X ramp execution. To summarize, the pullback reduces risk but remains dependent on high growth and architectural expansion.

The Make-or-Break Variable

The most important factor which could either make or break the success of Astera in the coming years is the execution concentration on the Scorpio-X product. In the coming two years, the proportion of the revenues generated from the scale-up switching products will increase, and if the ramp rate of the revenues generated from the Scorpio-X product slows down because of the delay in qualification from hyperscalers, timing, and competition, the revenues will slow down very fast.

Since the stock is already at a premium, a slight slowdown in execution will cause the multiple to go down again. However, this risk is mitigated to a certain degree by the high level of customer engagement, participation in multiple protocols, and the high level of switching costs associated with AI clusters when the products are qualified. Nevertheless, the timing issue cannot be eliminated, and the sensitivity will increase the impact of the timing issue.

Takeaway

I see the pullback in Astera is simply a slowdown in sequential growth and reinvesting their margins. There are no underlying issues with them. They are extremely embedded in the hyperscaler roadmap, and Scorpio-X is ready to increase the content per rack. They are cash-rich and debt-free with a tight relationship with AWS. Volatility is rising, but so is their architectural relevance.

data.title: Block: The Bitcoin Facade Hiding A Double-Digit Core Growth Story
data.description:
Overview

Although this is my first formal evaluation of Block, Inc. (XYZ), I have a lot of familiarity with the company and its products. The one thing that struck me as I was reviewing some numbers was Block's (apparently) anemic growth and surprisingly low cash generation—just 0.5% YoY revenue growth and 5.2% free cash flow margins over the past two years.

Just a few years ago, Block—formerly Square—was an up-and-coming fintech, firing on all cylinders. But some things have happened since then.

Block was a beneficiary of pandemic-driven tailwinds that saw consumers switch to digital payments. The company’s foray into Bitcoin—a major contributor to growth during the 2021 crypto bull run—now drags its bottom line. Add in decelerating user spending, sector uncertainty, increased competition (e.g., Toast (TOST) and Fiserv’s (FISV) Clover), and Afterpay integration costs—and you can begin to understand why XYZ trades where it does today.

But that’s in the past.

The article that follows zeroes in on Block’s current valuation (and what it implies about future expectations) and examines how realistic it is (or not) for the company to turn things around.

Q3 Performance

Block’s revenue growth is being dragged down by Bitcoin’s (BTC-USD) performance.

You see, Block allows Bitcoin purchases by acquiring the asset and reselling it to users for a tiny premium. Q3 Bitcoin revenue—$1.97 billion—was down 19% YoY (impacting its Cash App performance).

If you peel away Bitcoin-related volatility, Block’s core services (Square & Cash App) are growing in the double-digits.

For Square, Block’s “upmarket” strategy is paying off. Its 12.2% GPV growth was thanks to “mid-market” sellers—who now represent nearly half of Square’s total volume. And larger sellers tend to be more “sticky,” which boosts the platform’s revenue durability.

Cash App continues to be popular. Subscription and service revenue grew 22.6%—thanks mostly to the Cash App Card and Cash App Borrow.

Q4/FY25 Preview

Ahead of the February 26 reveal and beyond the obvious (e.g., revenue/EPS beats, 2026 guidance, volume growth, etc.), I’m watching how Block’s “Rule of 40” (defined as "gross profit growth + adjusted operating income margin") aspirations are shaping up.

Last year, Block's board approved a $5 billion increase to its share repurchase program. The actual number of shares retired in Q4 could clue us in as to how confident the company is in future cash generation.

Financial Trends

I’m going to start out by displaying Block’s revenue growth and free cash flow margin trends over the last eight quarters—bearing in mind that its recent performance has been negatively impacted by Bitcoin.

Now, Block's free cash flow margin of 22.9% in model Q8 (Q3 2025) was due to many reasons. For starters, Block’s net income is surging—a 62.7% YoY increase in Q3 2025—reaching $461.5 million. So, some of this is due to real progress on profitability. Then, there are high non-cash add-backs like stock-based compensation, or SBC (+$309.4 million), and settlement timing/working capital changes (+$601.9 million).

So while it was a meaningful improvement, Block is clearly nowhere near 20% (normalized). It’s probably closer to ~13%—even with SBC. And, of course, on a quarterly basis, we can still expect to see ebb and flow.

Taking a closer look at some operational trends I monitor, what really pops out to me is Block's top-line improvements.

Gross margins improving from 35.4% to 43.8% within a span of two years is laudable. Meanwhile, easier-to-adjust figures—like R&D and capital intensity—remain quite low. While this is good for the bottom line, low investments here have their drawbacks (R&D/capex expenditures are needed for innovation). In any event, it is quite apparent to me that Block is likely to reach its "Rule of 40" goal.

Valuation Assumptions

As for model inputs, $27 billion for "Year 1 Revenue" is the consensus revenue estimate for 2026. "Revenue Growth" of 5% and "FCF Margin" of 7.5% are my estimates. These are based on historical performance and what I think can be reasonably achievable for Block. 5% Revenue Growth feels like a nice “middle ground” that accounts for Bitcoin-related volatility. Block's "Beta" varies by source. Seeking Alpha has it at ~1.5, which feels right and places the discount rate just under 10%.

Most everything else is either standard DCF methodology (i.e., "Terminal Growth" of 2%) or was derived from Block's most recent financial statements.

Next, I ran a Sensitivity Analysis on my DCF results. My focus is on revenue growth and free cash flow margin.

Block is 2.4x more sensitive to margins than it is to growth. So, it’s wise of them to focus on margins.

Next, I tried to think of growth and margin levers—actions Block can take to move these assumptions.

One thing that interests me is "AI-driven efficiency." The companies I’m most interested in this year are the ones that can implement AI to improve operations without having a “capex drag.”

Block appears well-positioned to take advantage of AI without having to spend billions in infrastructure.

Then again, so do its competitors—PayPal (PYPL) and Toast. But I think there’s room for multiple players.

So where does Block fit in for investors interested in this space?

Block can be viewed as the “Goldilocks” of fintech—faster than PayPal but significantly cheaper than the “pure-growth” Toast.

Moat

With AI-related existential threats impacting the software industry valuations, it would be ignorant of me not to address “moat.” Many are wondering why AI is moving valuations in software so much—even when material impact still seems years down the road. The answer is multifactorial.

Generative AI and “agentic workflows” are lowering "barriers to entry" across software—making disruption more achievable. This erodes “terminal value” and increases the cost of equity for software across the board.

This is why—even as some SaaS companies are "beating and raising"–multiples are compressing.

For Block, its data advantage (proprietary transaction history) and integrations (i.e., Square Financial Services) will help insulate it from AI risks. AI can write a few hundred lines of code, but it can't open and run a bank, too! Of course, seamless integration of AI into its own products is crucial, too. Square AI, for instance, is becoming an “automated manager” for SMBs—handling things like inventory refills and staffing schedules.

Block doesn’t just have nightmares about robots, though. It has to deal with vertical disruption from the likes of Toast (restaurants) and Shopify (SHOP) (e-commerce) and Big Tech entry from the likes of Apple (AAPL) (“Tap to Pay on iPhone”).

Fortunately, over the years, Block has transformed from merely a “credit card reader” to an operating system. So, it's not as easy for businesses to leave Square (switching costs are high).

Final Words

After stripping away the Bitcoin-related facade, Block's double-digit core revenue growth and improving profitability look particularly strong for a company its size/age. My discounted cash flow model suggests that Block is priced as if it has reached terminal growth—but we know this is not yet the case. For those interested in fintech—who can also stand some volatility—XYZ looks like a good choice (initiate “Buy”) at these prices.

Risks to my thesis include AI disruption, competition, credit risk (Afterpay), and regulatory scrutiny (i.e., potential changes to interchange fee regulations).

data.title: UTG: What A Real 'Dividend Growth' Machine Looks Like
data.description:
Readers familiar with my investing philosophy know that I am a big fan of using high cash income investments to "create my own growth" through reinvesting and compounding. This tends to cause me to invest mostly in corporate credit asset classes, like senior secured loans, high-yield bonds, business development companies ("BDCs"), as well as other high-yielding assets (debt and equity) that generate interest or dividend income in the high single digits or even low double digits.

This allows me to target the typical historical average "equity return" of 9%-10% per annum without having to be dependent on capital gains, like investors in traditional equity, like the S&P 500, whose cash yield only averages about 1.5%. With such a low cash yield, typical equity "growth" investors need to achieve a capital gain averaging about 8% every year to make up the difference between 1.5% and their 9%-10% target return.

For me and many of my readers, investing in loans, bonds, and other consistently high-paying fixed-income assets, whose reinvested and compounded cash flows eliminate most or all of the need for capital gains, allows us to sleep better at night through volatile market periods, avoiding the angst of equity investors earning puny yields while waiting for markets to recover. The icing on our cake is our realization that when prices drop, even on our own assets (which has been happening lately!), as well as the market in general, we can grow our income at a faster rate than ever, since we can reinvest and compound at bargain prices, which means higher reinvestment yields.

UTG, A Fund That Does It All

The Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG) is a fund that lets us have it all: (1) a solid, steady cash flow that continues through thick and thin, all sorts of stock markets and economic cycles, plus (2) some serious income growth over the more than two decades since its inception in 2004.

To put some numbers on that:

  • UTG was started in 2004, 22 years ago this month;
  • Since that time it has increased its annual dividend 13 times, most recently last June.
  • Its total return has also been impressive:

The above chart is as of the end of 2025. When you add in the last month and a half, UTG has slightly beaten the S&P 500 in total return since its inception in 2004, with this current chart showing UTG in blue and SPY in red over that period (total return includes both dividends and capital gains/losses).

Wed 2004-02-25 to Fri 2026-02-13 OverallReturn
SPY × +791.68% +10.47%/yr
SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust Exchange-Traded Fund
UTG × +924.92%. +11.17%/yr
Reaves Utility IF Mutual Fund

Source: Total Real Returns.

Unlike the S&P 500 (SPY), however, UTG has generated its 11% average total return largely via its annual cash yield, which has ranged mostly between 6% and 7%, with the rest from capital gains in the 4%-5% per year range. As shown earlier, S&P's cash yield averages only about 1.5% per annum, so it is twice as dependent on capital gains to meet its historical total return target as UTG is to meet an equivalent total return level.

As its name suggests, UTG focuses on the utility and infrastructure sectors, describing its investment objective in similar language to many other closed-end funds:

The investment objective of the Fund is to provide a high level of after-tax total return consisting primarily of tax-advantaged dividend income and capital appreciation. It intends to invest at least 80% of its total assets in dividend-paying common and preferred stocks and debt instruments of companies within the utility industry. Up to 20% of the Fund may be invested in the securities of other industries.

When UTG says it seeks to generate "tax-advantaged dividend income," it isn't kidding. It consistently does, with between 95% and 100% of its dividends typically classified as "qualified" for income tax purposes. That means UTG is an ideal choice for accounts that are taxable, as well as for those that are tax-deferred, like IRAs.

UTG's portfolio, by industry, comprises:

  • Utilities 58.5%
  • Telecommunications 18.0%
  • Real Estate 7.9%
  • Energy, Oil & Gas 8.6%
  • Industrial, Capital Equipment 7.0%

The Reaves Asset Management team includes three managers, all of whom have been with the firm for over 20 years. Their stated investment philosophy is:

Reaves Asset Management believes that companies' characteristics drive their investment outcomes. Reaves favors companies which possess some, or all, of the following characteristics:

  • Operate in businesses with high barriers to entry

  • Face limited competition/higher regulatory scrutiny

  • Consistent, sustainable cash flow generation

  • Profitable in both up and down economic cycles

  • Dividends are viewed as an important component of capital allocation

This is very consistent with my own investment philosophy and would be difficult to disagree with. But more important, UTG has really executed on it and has the results to prove it, both recently and over the past 20+ years.

The reason UTG has been able to consistently increase its own dividend payout (13 times) while also continually boosting its NAV and market price over the past 22 years has been its ability to select investments (utilities and other companies) that, themselves, consistently increase their own dividends regularly.

The fund recently announced the dividend growth of its own portfolio holdings, and it was quite impressive:

  • As of 12/31/2025, UTG owned 64 common stocks, 51 of which paid a cash dividend in Q4 2025. In the past 12 months, 44 of these companies increased their dividend, four maintained the same payment, one company initiated a dividend, and two companies reduced their dividend. The overall weighted average dividend increase of UTG's investment portfolio was 6.2%.
  • At the end of 2024, UTG owned 55 stocks, and 44 of those increased their dividend that year, for an adjusted weighted average dividend increase of 7.2%. It says in a footnote that it was actually much higher, but because one of their investments, PG&E Corp., increased its dividend by 150%, they didn't include it in calculating the average. That suggests to me the fund, in addition to its other attributes, must be somewhat of a "real Boy Scout" in its reporting, which I like.
  • In 2023, UTG owned 56 stocks, of which 48 increased their dividends, for a weighted average increase overall of 7.9%. They "fess up" in a footnote that the percentage increase number includes a 100% increase from one firm, Constellation Energy. They say that if that were excluded from their calculation, the average increase would have "only" been 6%.

Bottom Line

Given its fine record, it's no surprise UTG has been on my radar as well as in my portfolio for many years. The market, recognizing its solid performance, has maintained its price near par (i.e., NAV) or at a small premium for a number of years. Currently, it has dropped to a discount just slightly below NAV, so this may be as attractive a time as ever to create or build positions. I look for long-term opportunities, not for trading vehicles, and believe UTG has proven itself to be a real "keeper."

data.title: SCHG's Upgrade Engine Is Losing Steam - Time To Temper Expectations
data.description:
The last time I wrote about the Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG), I had benchmarked its expected performance against VUG and rated it a Hold. My arguments were that SCHG is slower to adapt and more thematically anchored to tech. That made it less positioned for a regime of valuation pressure I saw ahead. That part of SCHG's thesis has played out as expected. VUG has held up better in a period of consolidation since. And the difference is a few basis points - again that is the extent of arbitrage that should be expected given the broader growth alignment and a lot of commonality between the two ETFs. I do not expect the SCHG underperformance to grow beyond a couple of percentage points for context over a longer consolidation or turning regime.

This thesis is not on the comparative view but on SCHG's own outlook ahead as the volatile consolidation phase continues, in my view. In my earlier thesis too, I had formed the view around long-term growth prospects staying intact but broad tech-led multiple expansion slowing and returns becoming more selective rather than driven by widespread rerating. This thesis gauges the market situation through a raw data lens (earnings upgrades) and argues why SCHG's path ahead remains a Hold, with another round of quarterly results reported since my last thesis.

Earnings Upgrade Momentum

I have collated the 6-month and 3-month earnings revision trends from Seeking Alpha analyst estimates/revisions and checked whether the 6-month upgrade trend is materially higher than the most recent 3-month period (for FY1). The assumption is that it should support a hypothesis that the latest earnings cycle is weaker than the prior ones, i.e., upgrade momentum is decelerating. This analysis looks at the top 8 holdings in SCHG that together account for ~55% of the portfolio.

Across all the mega-cap stocks, the revisions we have seen in the past 3 months have been weaker than the levels we were seeing in the prior period. The only exception to that claim is Tesla, but revisions there are weak anyway. Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Apple Inc. (AAPL), and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) are still seeing some meaningful upgrades, but nowhere near the earlier levels.

Using FY2 as an outer year growth proxy, we see similar trends. This is not truly a great long-duration proxy, but it is the most practical given the amount of analyst coverage it gets (compared to, say, 2030). Here too, apart from Meta, all other mega caps have been seeing a slower pace of earnings upgrades than we were seeing in the 6-month period. Revisions are still healthy; they simply show cooling trends and dispersion within the mega-cap group, not a panic button, but neither a euphoric rally supportive signal.

Emphasis On Earnings Upgrade

Another angle I wanted to test using this data is whether earnings upgrades are effective in keeping the markets excited as much as they were in the peak of the 2025 rally. To do that, the table below compares revisions to Y1 and Y2 (an early proxy for long-duration outer years) in the past 3 months and share price returns in the same period.

Three stocks need discussion here—Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Alphabet, and Broadcom Inc. (AVGO). These are the three stocks with the maximum earnings upgrades in either Y1 or Y2 (combined). Of these, both Google and Meta have seen relative strength in share prices. Google clearly has stronger revisions across both years. Meta also sees material earnings revisions in Y2 (although Y1 is weak). Meta also has not been trading near the highs, unlike the rest of the pack, so that helps share prices too. Broadcom has remained subdued despite healthy upgrades, and this can be attributed to a slowing upgrade momentum (discussed in the preceding section).

All in all, earnings upgrades do seem to be an important factor in determining share prices in the current regime, especially after a bull rally in 2025. And healthy upgrades alone are not enough; markets need a positive momentum in these upgrades to start rewarding the stocks. In today's environment, where upgrade acceleration is selective and becoming rarer, the odds of markets staying in a dispersion and digestion phase become higher (rather than broader rerating across the board).

Implications

The analysis has limitations. It looks at short-term tactical data (3-month, 6-month revision trends). There are other factors at play. Such as CapEx in the case of Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), dragging sentiment (although that can be traced eventually to slowing earnings revision for FY1 and FY2 as seen), or recent corrections in Meta (that drove its relative 3-month outperformance, rather than earnings revisions alone). Narratives are also very different across the universe of analysis.

Despite the limitations, a slowing earnings upgrade momentum cannot be denied from the data we have. Equally, positive upgrades (as well as upgrade momentum) seem to have some impact on share prices. And the conclusions, if not applicable at the stock level across the board, still broadly uphold my hypothesis that they do coincide with what is shaping up as a market where dispersion and consolidation (or a slower grind up) is more likely.

Importantly, this thesis does not aim to imply a collapse. Earnings revisions are still broadly positive. The thesis tries to highlight a tougher time ahead for SCHG, with more than half the portfolio under pressure and looking for a re-acceleration in earnings revisions. Within the rest of the portfolio, the software plays are anyway reeling under an investor apathy for long-duration assumptions in an environment where the risks of AI commoditizing several software products are being debated.

What I see ahead in this environment is earnings growing into valuations, rather than leading to meaningful forward revisions. The best-case scenario for share prices in that case is consolidation. Hence, SCHG remains a Hold. I will await another round of earnings and see whether they re-accelerate earnings upgrades before jumping back in. Long-term investors may accumulate through an expected phase of consolidation, but the risk-reward balance does not imply an urgent need for fresh investments at this stage.

data.title: Provident Financial Services: Past Monumental Upside Justifies A Bit More From Here
data.description:
One of the strongest prospects that I have picked out over the last couple of years now has been Provident Financial Services (PFS). Back in November of last year, for instance, I reaffirmed the company as a ‘buy’ candidate. This was driven by continued growth when it came to deposits and loans, as well as high asset quality. Add on top of this how cheap the stock was when it came to earnings, book value, and tangible book value, and I believed that further upside was likely. Since then, shares have jumped 28.9%. That's well above the 5% increase that the S&P 500 achieved over the same window of time. But that pales in comparison to the real story. Since I originally rated it a ‘buy’ in February of 2024, the stock has surged 65.8%. That's meaningfully above the 38% rise that the market enjoyed.

At some point, a company that outperforms the market to this extent does not justify further upside. I don't think we are there just yet. However, I would argue that we are getting close. In fact, based on valuation alone, this is the point at which I would normally downgrade to a more neutral rating. But when you add on top of this continued growth on the balance sheet and high asset quality, I believe that a bit more upside is probable. Because of this, I believe that maintaining it as a soft ‘buy’ candidate is the appropriate move.

It’s Too Early to Bank on a Downgrade

The newest data that investors have regarding Provident Financial Services came out in late January. That data covered the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. According to management, deposits came in at $19.28 billion. That compared favorably to the $19.10 billion reported for the third quarter. And it represented a respectable increase over the $18.62 billion the company reported a year earlier. It is important to note that not all deposits are equal. Brokered deposits, which are deposits borrowed from other financial institutions, are some of the least desirable. This is because of how expensive they are. And sure enough, year over year, brokered deposits did increase by $253.6 million. But even with that stripped out, we would have gotten a rise in organic deposits. So I view this, on the whole, as a positive.

The value of loans has also been rising. At the end of 2024, the company had loans amounting to $18.63 billion. But today, that number is $19.33 billion. Largely, the company focuses its efforts here on two main categories. The largest of these would be commercial loans. About $7.40 billion of its entire loan portfolio fell under this category. A fairly distant second would be the $4.84 billion worth of commercial loans that the company has. The only other categories that are at or above the $1 billion mark would be the multifamily category at $3.67 billion, followed by the $1.97 billion of residential mortgage loans. Even though the enterprise does have certain areas of concentration, the fact that it is still diverse all the same is encouraging.

We definitely should not ignore other aspects of the balance sheet. For instance, Provident Financial Services has about $3.47 billion invested in securities. A year earlier, this amounted to $3.12 billion. Cash and cash equivalents over that same time went from $205.9 million to $211.5 million. Debt, meanwhile, actually changed from $2.44 billion to $2.52 billion. What all of this tells me is that management doesn't have an issue taking on a bit of additional debt in order to boost exposure to certain assets. Primarily, I am talking about loans and securities.

The end result of these changes in the composition of the balance sheet, as well as the declining interest rate environment that made deposits more affordable, was an increase in net interest margin from 3.28% to 3.44%. That was instrumental in pushing net interest income up from $172.9 million in the final quarter of 2024 to $198.6 million in the final quarter of 2025. It also helped that the company saw a $1.2 million benefit for credit losses as opposed to the $7 million provision reported the previous year. The strong showing in the final quarter was helpful in pushing overall net interest income for the year up to $757 million compared to the $513.1 million reported for 2024.

Non-interest income, meanwhile, has been growing also. The final quarter of the year saw a reading of $28.3 million. That stacked up nicely compared to the $24.2 million reported a year earlier. According to the company, this improvement came mostly from greater wealth management income and a rise in bank-owned life insurance revenue. A roughly $0.6 million increase stemming from gains on securities transactions certainly helped in a big way also. Regardless, this and the rise in net interest income proved useful in pushing net profits for the company up from $48.5 million the previous year to $83.4 million last year. And for the year as a whole, the $291.2 million net income generated by the company exceeded the $115.5 million reported a year earlier.

If we use the data provided by management for 2025, we can see in the chart above that the stock is now trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of 10.3. That's a bit above the 10 maximum I typically look for, but it's not so high that I would be turned off by that alone. In fact, relative to the five banks that I compared our prospect to in that chart, it is still trading quite cheap. Only one of the competitors is cheaper than it is. In the chart below, you can see a ranking that is similar when it comes to the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book multiples. In both instances, three of the five competitors are more expensive than Provident Financial Services is.

Valuation is important, as is growth. But there are other important considerations also. Asset quality is one good example. The chart below shows a return on assets reading for the company of 1.34%. That compares nicely to the 1% minimum I typically look for. It also puts the company above four of the five banks that I stacked it up against. In the subsequent chart below that, you can see the exact same relative ranking for the company, with four of the enterprises worse off than it is when it comes to return on equity. What's more, the 11.78% reading that we get here beats out the 10% minimum that I typically look for.

One last thing that I would like to touch on briefly would be overall credit quality. According to the company, during the quarter, 0.40% of its loans were classified as non-performing. That is below the 0.50% maximum I typically look for. Furthermore, about 0.32% of assets fell under the non-performing designation. Once again, I look for a reading here of no more than 0.50%. So the business certainly checks off that box.

Takeaway

When you add all of the data together, it's difficult not to remain optimistic about Provident Financial Services. Most certainly, the easy money has been captured by this point. But that doesn't mean that further upside is impossible or unlikely. In fact, given the totality of the circumstances, with growth continuing, asset quality and credit quality both coming in strong, and more, I believe that maintaining it as a soft ‘buy’ is the right choice.

data.title: Q4 Healthcare Dividends: Johnson & Johnson Kept Outshining Pfizer (Rating Upgrades)
data.description:
PFE Vs. JNJ Stock: Q4 2025 Updates

I last covered Pfizer Inc. stock (PFE) on January 6, 2026, with an article titled "Pfizer: 2026 Guidance Points To Continued Margin And Growth Pressure.” The article focused on the updates the company announced for 2026 and rated the stock as a sell. As for Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), my last publication dated back to November 7, 2025. That article was triggered by its then-new dividend declaration and rated the stock as a hold. Since those articles, the share prices of these two healthcare stalwarts have changed considerably, as seen in the chart below.

Besides the stock price changes, a few fundamental catalysts have also emerged. These changes include the operation updates both companies provided in their FQ4 earnings reports (ER) for 2025. In addition, given the role of dividends for both stocks’ investment thesis, their latest dividend declarations are also noteworthy. The rest of this article will examine the changes more closely and perform a holistic comparison between JNJ and PFE. In the end, my takeaways are twofold. First, I now see better prospects for both companies and thus upgrade my rating for both by one notch. I now rate JNJ as a buy and PFE as a hold. Second, among these two healthcare stocks, I keep seeing a better return/risk profile from JNJ for reasons detailed next. For those who cannot wait, my final scorecard for both companies is shown below.

PFE Vs. JNJ Stock: Valuation

Let me start with valuation. With the price changes mentioned above and their updated EPS guidance in the Q4 ER, JNJ now trades at a vast valuation premium compared to PFE. As shown in the chart below, JNJ is now priced with an FY1 P/E ratio of 21.1x, compared to PFE’s 9.22x FY1 P/E. As such, PFE is far more attractively priced with less than half of JNJ’s P/E ratio. Next, I will show that PFE’s valuation remains attractive also when adjusted for growth rate and dividend yield.

PFE Vs. JNJ Stock: Dividend Growth

This leads me to their latest dividend declarations. In tandem with their Q4 reports, JNJ and Pfizer have also announced their latest dividend payouts recently. There is not too much surprise at Johnson & Johnson. As shown in the next chart, it maintained its payout of $1.30 per share with a February 24, 2026, ex-dividend date. This represents a YOY increase of +4.8% compared to the payouts made in February 2025 and continues JNJ’s incredible track record of dividend raises (for 63 years, as seen in the third chart below).

The announcement made by Pfizer, in contrast, contains a bit more for me. PFE decided to maintain its quarterly dividend of $0.43 for the first quarter in 2026. The pause of dividend increases reflects stabilizing earnings on the positive side. But on the negative side, the company effectively interrupted its dividend increase track record. As a background, as seen in the third chart below, before this pause, the company had been building a decent dividend track record with 15 years of consecutive raises.

With these changes, JNJ now demonstrates far more robust dividend growth rates and consistency than PFE. For example, JNJ’s three-year compounded annual dividend growth rate is 4.92%, more than double PFE's 2.23%. Also note that the payout ratio is also lower for JNJ (at 47.59%) than PFE (at 53.25%) despite the growth divergence.

PFE And JNJ's PEGY ratios

Before moving away from dividends, it wouldn’t be fair if I didn’t point out that Pfizer’s dividend yield is far more than JNJ’s (6.28% vs. 2.14% on a FWD basis). Such a large difference in yield not only means more current income but also has implications for the overall return/risk ratio of both stocks per the following insights from Peter Lynch:

For dividend stocks, Lynch uses a revised version of the PEG ratio - the PEGY ratio, which is defined as the P/E ratio divided by the sum of the earnings growth rate and dividend yield. The idea behind the PEGY is very simple and effective (most effective ideas are simple). If a stock pays out a large part of its earnings as dividends, then investors do not need a high growth rate to enjoy healthy returns. And vice versa. And similar to the PEG ratio, his preference is a PEGY ratio of 1x or below.

Following Lynch’s above wisdom, the table below shows my calculations of the PEGY ratios for JNJ and PFE based on their latest dividend declarations and EPS revisions. Here, I used the 3-year dividend growth rates to approximate their earnings growth rates. The PEGY ratio is about 2.99x for JNJ and only 1.08x for PFE. As such, PFE’s current yield and lower P/E were able to offset the slower growth rates, making the stock’s valuation attractive both relative to JNJ and also relative to Lynch’s ideal threshold of a 1x PEGY ratio.

PFE Vs. JNJ Stock: Profitability Outlook

A key impression I formed while reading their Q4 ER is the improved profitability prospects (which is a key factor that catalyzed my rating upgrades for both stocks). As a reflection, the table below shows the latest profit margins for both JNJ and PFE. As seen, both boast strong and also very comparable margins across all a key metrics in my view.

Looking ahead, I see a few ongoing catalysts for both companies to continue, or even further augment, their margins. JNJ has received some positive news on the legal front regarding its talc case (see the news release before for more details). For PFE, the latest development of vaccine policy (see the news release before for more details) is also a net positive in my view and provides the much-needed clarity for the company’s future.

Seeking Alpha News (Jan 30): A New Jersey federal judge has dismissed a lawsuit that claimed Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) engaged in fraud by using bankruptcy to settle tens of thousands of cases alleging that its talc-based baby powder and other products caused cancer.

Seeking Alpha News (Feb 14): Changes to U.S. vaccine policy under RFK Jr. challenged in court. Several leading medical groups in the U.S. urged a federal judge on Friday to bar the CDC from holding its next vaccine advisory meeting and block the agency from implementing its recently upended immunization guidelines.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

Inventory is an aspect most dividend investors ignore. However, we found it extremely effective, especially for companies at the two extremes of the cyclicality: those that are highly cyclical (like semiconductor companies) and those that are super stable (like JNJ and PFE), per the insights from Peter Lynch:

To start, unlike many other financial data that are more open to interpretation, inventory is one of the less ambiguous financial data. Lynch also explained why inventory levels can be a telltale sign of business cycles. Especially for cyclical businesses, inventory buildup is a warning sign, which indicates the company (or sector) might be overproducing while the demand is already softening. Conversely, depleting inventory could be an early sign of a recovery.

In the case of JNJ, the company’s DIO (days of inventory outstanding) has been very stable, as seen in the chart below. Its DIO has hovered near its long-term average of 151 days, a demonstration of the stable demand for its products and also the effectiveness of its management. In contrast, PFE’s DIO exhibits more volatility and also an increasing trend indicating inventory buildup.

Finally, the latest dividend declarations JNJ and PFE made (continued growth vs. paused growth), when combined with their share repurchases (see the next chart below), also suggest far more flexible capital allocation choices at JNJ. To wit, JNJ was able to maintain a rather consistent Net Common Buyback Yield over the years, with disruptions around 2023 and 2024 due to the reconstruction of the business. In contrast, Pfizer has completely stopped its share repurchases since 2023.

To close, this reexamination of PFE and JNJ is mainly triggered by three new catalysts since my last writings: their FQ4 ERs, their latest dividend declarations, and the new developments on the legal/policy front. An examination of these changes has led me to see A) an improved profit outlook for both stocks and B) a more skewed return/risk profile from JNJ than PFE after a holistic consideration encapsulated in the scorecard below. PFE’s single-digit P/E and high yield could be appealing for value or yield hunters. However, I see better odds for JNJ to keep delivering stronger total returns, especially on a risk-adjusted basis.

data.title: This Is A Stock Picker's Market
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Next-gen investing experts Julia Ostian, Jack Bowman, and Kenio Fontes share their investment strategies (0:30) Rare earths and precious metals (2:20) Amazon, Apple, AMD and tech - concerns and excitement (9:00) Portfolio allocations (41:00)

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Welcome back to Investing Experts Podcast. Very, very excited to welcome back to the show Julia Ostian, Kenio Fontes, and Jack Bowman, three outstanding analysts we are fortunate enough to have on Seeking Alpha. They've been on the podcast before with Rob Isbitts. I'm very, very happy to have them back talking markets, talking stocks, talking whatever they want pretty much. Everybody, welcome to the show. It's great to have you.

Julia, we'll start with you. Do you want to introduce yourself and just remind listeners what you write about, where you write, things like that, your investment strategy.

Julia Ostian: Definitely. Hey, everyone. So I'm Julia. I started writing for Seeking Alpha pretty much a year ago, almost. And I'm mostly writing covering the tech sector and AI, of course, I guess everyone is interested in AI right now. And I'm not an exception.

I cover different sectors as well. But I would say probably the tech sector fascinates me the most. And I get the most excited about learning how things work, how companies progress, how they find new innovative ways to make money and that's really interesting and find new innovative ways to make money. And that's really interesting.

My investing strategy actually is focused heavily on momentum, on sentiment of the market.

I really believe that especially right now in this cycle, momentum is really, really important and ahead of Nvidia (NVDA) earnings, for example, right now, I really think that the company can show 60% revenue growth and the stock can tank just because people will be nervous or just because people wanted to see more or wanted more from this company.

So I am a true believer in the sentiment moving with the market. I trade sometimes here and there. I don't always hold my positions for a long time.

For example, right now I really think that the company can show 60% revenue growth and the stock can tank just because people will be nervous or just because people wanted to see more or wanted more from this company. So I am a true believer in this sentiment and moving with the market. trade sometimes here and there. I don't always hold.

Rena Sherbill: Much appreciated. Kenio, would you share with us how you invest, what you're looking at?

Kenio Fontes: Of course, thanks for having me here again. Kenio Fontes, Brazil based investor and I invest in a bit of everything with a focus on tech and retail but I'm always keeping on any stock that I find interesting and that can offer a compelling story.

I'm mostly looking for long-term investing, like 10 maybe 20 years from here. Of course I do some trades when stocks get too overvalued and mainly buy based on sentiment like Julia said but with a different focus.

I buy when the market is very pessimistic about something like Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) a few years ago or Amazon (AMZN) right now. I think that's pretty much my investing style

Rena Sherbill: Jack Bowman?

Jack Bowman: Thanks, so I'm probably known as the wonkiest person on our panel. I write a lot about macro and I do a lot of my investing strategy around macroeconomics. And so I'm making portfolio adjustments based on what the Fed is talking about, based on where GDP is going, and that's most of my trading.

Though I do some stock picking here and there. I like to dabble in material stocks like critical minerals and Rare Earths have been one of my focus lately. And then of course, I have some coverage of tech stocks because everyone's gotta love their tech stocks.

And I'm sure that we will talk about Amazon in just a moment because that has been on all of our minds, I'm sure.

Rena Sherbill: Jack, I was quoting you for a bunch of episodes about your take on Amazon and how Prime is not their moneymaker and AWS and building brand and what that means. And so, yeah, I've loved your guys' takes on Amazon.

Jack, I can get, why are you focused on Rare Earths or what led you to your choice of focus there brand and what that means and so yeah I've loved your guys's takes on Amazon. if I can get why are you focused on rare earths or what is what led you to your choice?

Jack Bowman: Yeah, so there has been maybe the last 30 years, this kind of sense in the macro environment of like the end of history. The Cold War ended, the Soviet Union was gone, and everything could be solved with globalization. Couldn't pay a domestic worker, pay a foreign worker. Couldn't buy domestic products, buy foreign products. And we did this to the extreme in rare earths.

And we woke up maybe two years ago realizing that China controls 90 % of rare earth processing. So we just can't make, like the amount of graphite and the amount of cobalt and things, we just can't process it here, but we still need it.

So now we're facing a world where in a trade war with China, they're weaponizing that against us. You don't want to play ball with us, you want to raise tariffs? Well, no rare earths for you. And that suddenly threw a giant wrench in our supply chains. And it's meant huge opportunity for domestic rare earth companies and Canadian rare earth companies in particular have been doing very well as we kind of re-industrialize that part of the sector that we let just kind of languish for a long, long time.

Julia Ostian: I have to say I really like this industry as well and I actually rely on Jack's articles for this myself. A year ago I have been in Indonesia and I like first hand I saw how China invests just loads of money in different countries to lock in those rare earths like everything basically anything they can get their hands on in different countries to lock in those rare earths like every two.

Basically anything they can get their hands on they will be locking in and I think it's a very very important topic and I think the US will really have a shortage in a couple of years when China feels more comfortable with like telling the US that they will be locking in and I think it's a very, very important topic and I think the US will really have a shortage in a couple of years when China feels more comfortable with telling the US that, listen guys, so now we have something that we also want from you, not only you want from us.

So yeah, I think it's a fascinating part of the market as well.

Rena Sherbill: It also sounds like shades of the AI with China and America competing and piggybacking and being exclusionary and yeah, interesting. Any companies that you would highlight or lowlight in the rare earths just as we're getting into things?

Jack Bowman: Yeah, the ultimate winner of all of this push so far with government meddling, so the US government has done a lot of meddling so far in the market. But they're buying up stockpiles now, they're trying to pass bills so they can just preemptively buy things.

They passed a floor where, so MP Materials (MP) is one of my big picks in this sector. They secured a contract with the Pentagon. The Pentagon buys these magnet components at almost double the going price on the broad market.

So MPs digging up stuff at $30 a ton. They're able to sell it for $50 a ton to the open market or to the government for $100 a ton. And this has created this kind of unfair part of the market where they're able to out-compete everybody on their margins because no one has a buyer like MP does.

(LAC), Lithium Americas is the other big one. They also have a government contract now. And they're one of the few Western companies producing lithium, which is a huge deal in battery production, as we may know.

Kenio Fontes: Can I ask a question for Jack? Do you have an opinion on gold? I wrote down an article about gold miners, I think it's a month ago, and it was very debatable, because my take was, I'm an individual investor, I'm not a head fund manager, so I don't think I have to beat the S&P (SP500) in the next quarter just in the next decade or five years so i'm not willing to bet that Barrick (B) and all these gold miners will be selling gold for $5k in the next year or two years, three years so I can be patient.

So what's your take on gold and all this momentum style versus long-term thinking?

Jack Bowman: Yeah, gold has been exceptional. It's been great for my portfolio, because I was a gold bug before all this, but now I'm looking at it saying, hey, we've run up so much. I saw a photo of a strip mall in China that had silver prices at $136, because they have a premium there. And it was like the most exuberant I had ever seen a precious metals market.

And so at this point, I'm not adding to my precious metals anymore. I'm sitting and waiting, which is a weird thing for me to say, because I have been banging the table for years about how much everyone needs gold, and all the investors in America are like, eschewing gold and refusing.

I think among miners right now, there's still some opportunity because they've lagged gold, and there's that fear of like, well, what if it falls back down to 3000, and now they can't sell for 5000 anymore? That's a big risk. And then the other thing is that there's, I think, a lot of geopolitical risk in a lot of these miners that don't do business in the US or Canada.

And so it would really, I think, come down to a lot of their individual. I know B2G (BTG) had a big deal with, Julia, what was that country that they were in? It was somewhere in West Africa.

Julia Ostian: In Mali. Yeah, they had a huge problem.

Jack Bowman: In Mali. And the government kicked them out.

Rena Sherbill: Okay. Anything else before we get into the tech side of things, which we've seen have some momentum after some? Well, there's just volatility, right? There's just volatility afoot. so I guess, what would you say about, are, Julia mentioned that Nvidia earnings are coming up next week. We also saw many of the biggest tech players report in recent weeks. We've had some nice takeaways from earnings season.

I guess, Kenio, let's start with you. What's top of mind for you when you look at tech? If you want to specify Amazon, feel free to or any other stocks. But in general, how are you looking at that part of the market?

Kenio Fontes: I feel like it's a great time for stock picking like we are not technically in a bear market because the index fell like 5 or 4 percent but some tech stocks were beating down like 20, 30, 40 % if we are talking about any video based on the all time highs. So I feel like it's a great time to go contrary and invest like in quality names, but in a cheap price. I think Amazon is my number one priority right now.

A couple quarters ago, I don't feel comfortable enough to increase my position, but right now I do because the sentiment about what market is feeling about Amazon it's just short term.

CapEx, of course, will put some pressure on the cash flow in the next one, maybe two, maybe three years. But I think the ROI will be very, very, very good, enhance the EPS, the compounding in the next decade. They invest in robots, AI chips. It's not only AWS capacity or an AI race. It makes sense. So I think we can benefit from this market euphoria.

Rena Sherbill: What would you say most concerns you when you're looking at Amazon or other tech names that you like? What has you kind of worried the most?

Kenio Fontes: Probably regulation. I mean Amazon is the company like too big to fail So I think some countries some regions may feel like We should stop Amazon.

I think this is my number one risk in the long term not of course unions and all this, things like layoffs all of this I think can impact in the long term not only AWS because I'm not concerned if they lose 1% of market share to Azure or GCP I think the main long-term risk is regulation and all of that, not the market share because it's a very, very wide-moat company.

Julia Ostian: I have actually just downgraded Amazon. It had been my top pick for a long time. Yeah, I had a strong buy and I downgraded it to buy and it was like the reaction of the market of the people that read me was not probably the nicest because everyone was like, no, but you were like, they were the one telling us that Amazon should go up.

But yeah, I actually I got pretty disappointed this time around with Amazon not people are usually concerned about the capex, right and Amazon just presented an enormous capex.

But this was not the point of why I actually decided to downgrade this stock. I really did not like the free cashflow situation of Amazon because it went down three times in the last year from Q4 2024 to Q4 2025. And it's a lot for Amazon to cover the capex that was back then. And if we take into account that Amazon wants to increase it more than 50 % from here.

I'm not sure they really understand how to cover that cost and together with this day fire people and I think they want those fire rings and this money to cover Partly the capex which definitely should not be enough.

So all of these signals and I'm just not sure and also I compare it to the return on invested capital of Amazon to other Mag 7, for example, and Amazon stands at about 15 or 16 percent, which is okay because their weighted average cost of capital is at about 10 or 11. It's okay. This company does okay. But it doesn't do that good to invest 200 billion dollars in year in CapEx, in my opinion.

So here the worry is actually not the CapEx itself, but where it actually goes and how the company will be able to pay for this. Because with other Mag 7, for example, I'm not worried. They've got the money. So even if they overbuild the capacity, they can cover it.

They can use this capacity themselves and they will be fine. But with Amazon, I don't know. I just got nervous, so I downgraded it and I'm not buying on this dip or whatever. I'm not adding to my position, although I'm not selling also.

Rena Sherbill: So you'd consider it a hold at this point?

Julia Ostian: Yeah, yeah, for now, for now I'm holding and I'm pretty happy with where I am right now. If the company continues with this, with the layoffs and with the CapEx increase, I might trim even, but not yet.

I just want to see for a couple more quarters where it goes.

Jack Bowman: The Amazon layoffs don't really surprise me because I've been watching them build an army of robots.

And I mean this literally, there was this beautiful chart that Ark put out, part of their big ideas presentation that was showing the amount of people at Amazon and the amount of robots at Amazon. And you can see that the amount of people is very slowly dwindling. It peaked in 2021, but the amount of robots is still hitting all time highs, right?

And we're getting close to where in maybe five years, there may be more robots than people at Amazon.

Kenio Fontes: Yeah, they achieved like a million robots last year so that's a lot.

Jack Bowman: And so I'm seeing some of this of like, well, maybe we can just automate the entire warehouse top to bottom, right? Maybe we don't need as many people as we thought we did. That's a scary thought for the larger economy and the labor market.

Kenio Fontes: Yeah and what's the impact on margin on bottom line like triple I don't know double at least in the next 10 years?

Jack Bowman: Yeah, humans cost a lot of money, right? Way more than robots.

Kenio Fontes: Yeah, it's a strange thing to think, but...

Rena Sherbill: Well, at the risk of wanting to put all of our heads under our desks in cowering fear of what's to come.

Julia, I'm interested, you mentioned that your subscribers were questioning your change in thesis on Amazon. Did it surprise you as well? Was it, did you need like a quarter or two or a couple announcements in consecutive release that forced you into a slightly more bearish stance. And then I guess also, how do you communicate that to your subscribers in terms of a change in thesis?

Julia Ostian: It's a great question because I think people or some people, not all of them, but I think people tend to fall in love with companies and it can sound weird, but people really get into the business. They really learn about the management.

They really learn about how it goes. they basically decide that they will stick in no matter what happens. So for me, it's not like that. And I'm very simple with letting it go. If I see that I don't like something, I change my opinion in a second. I don't need to wait. I don't need to double check it for a few more times.

For example, if something drastic, if something horrible happens, right? If something that I'm not sure about, like with Amazon in this case, I just, could downgrade and say that I'm cautious and this is not what I was expecting and I can just wait and see for a couple of more quarters but I think it's a big problem in general of people they want to not to analyze what's going on, they want to believe what they heard in the first place.

So I'm not surprised. I take it as what it is I just show my opinion I just explain how I view all of this market and wait and see for a couple of more quarters and my thought processing probably, my decision making, and people can just take it for whatever it is.

Rena Sherbill: And I think to your point, a lot of people's resistance, I would imagine, is just the fear of change. People do not like change. And to your point that you're easy with change and that feels like something that's not a challenge for you is probably why you're good at investing.

Julia Ostian: Probably, yeah.

I think that's like a big thing is that people are scared to change their mind. They're scared to doubt themselves like all of those just human nature things come into play and you have to be super disciplined or naturally disciplined to see that through.

Julia Ostian: Definitely, yeah.

Rena Sherbill: Jack, anything else to add on the Amazon story?

Jack Bowman: So I think that the one thing that kind of ties us all together is like the current issue with software.

Amazon is running up into this now as a model provider as well. Of this fear that software is being eaten by AI and that AI CapEx is going to eventually build out enough capacity to replace most software orgs with some sort of in-house solution or whatever and cause them all to drop off, right? Salesforce (CRM) is down like 40% off of its highs, among others. Adobe (ADBE) is getting slaughtered and a couple others. ServiceNow (NOW) is a big one.

And I have been kind of pushing back on this in some ways of like, it's bled over into Microsoft (MSFT), into Amazon. And I don't think that the fear is well placed. I think there's a lot of like AI exuberance still in the air of it can solve everything. It is an omnipresent tool that can be immensely useful forever.

And I just don't think that's the case. I think we're going to run into a wall at some point with the market of we're going to either have to produce the gains that were, you got to spend money to make money. So we do have to eventually actually make money on all of this spending.

And then also we have to prove its usefulness. Software isn't dead if AI never becomes as useful as we think it might. And I think a lot of us are kind of putting the blinders on for that trade of like software's dead and AI's king and we can forget about it, but I don't think that's true.

Kenio Fontes: Yeah, and I think we have to mention the overreaction, not only about Amazon, but about whether the money is going like, Walmart is trading at 45 times earnings or 50 times earnings. Like it's a 70 % premium related to Amazon. So I think it's just too much, like the market is just so with so much fear about Amazon, but so confident about Walmart (WMT). it's both good companies, but t's just too different, you know?

Jack Bowman: Yeah, one's not covering the planet in data centers.

Kenio Fontes: Yeah, it's inflation linked but they are growing in the low single digits. So how can you trade it like 50 times earnings? Come on.

Rena Sherbill: Kenio, do you have a price target on Amazon or do you do price targets?

Kenio Fontes: I don't do much about price target, it's much more based on margin of safety. Kenio Fontes: I think FOMO is an important thing to say here. I think we should avoid FOMO. Like Jack said, we have seen some rotation and us, individual investors, we don't need to buy gold right now.

Jack Bowman: I think the one last thing I have to say about this is like among stock picking and especially for those people who are kind of bargain hunting in software right now, which is where I'm at, like to Kenio's point of like I'm not buying (IGV), the software ETF, too much risk for me.

Kenio Fontes: I think a better quote would be by Peter Lynch, buy business you understand, not you like, but at least you understand. I think it's better.

Jack Bowman: Yeah.

Rena Sherbill: I'm going to leave everybody with the final word and where everybody can find your insight, your analysis, perhaps your YouTube videos. Jack, I'll start with you since we just finished with you. Happy for you to share that with our audience.

Kenio Fontes: Yeah, most of my work is on Seeking Alpha So just follow me on seeking often and you will see every stock I talked about here Uber, Nvidia, Amazon everything you'll find on seeking alpha in my profile

Julia Ostian: As for me, besides of course of my Seeking Alpha, you can contact me on LinkedIn. I sometimes chat there with followers from Seeking Alpha. They ask me questions and I usually answer. Although I would say that on Seeking Alpha I write more frequently and there you can definitely find a wide range of companies and YouTube is like only some picks that the YouTube audience likes.

Rena Sherbill: Appreciate it very much. Julia Ostian, Kenio Fontes, and Jack Bowman, really, really happy to have you back on the podcast. And for followers or those that want to ask questions, feel free to leave us a comment. We hope to have all three back on at least once a quarter. So look forward to that. Thanks, everybody, for your time and deep dives today.
