data.title: Figma: Strong Results, Topping Expectations
data.description:
Web-based design and product development platform Figma, Inc. (FIG), released better-than-expected Q4 results, and investors are taking notice.

The company went public in mid-2025 to much anticipation and excitement. Priced at $33/share, FIG stock soared on the IPO date to the mid-$130/share range. The stock has since fallen back to earth, with shares now trading below its IPO price.

For its first public fiscal year, the company reported a loss with positive cash flow while maintaining a healthy balance sheet. FIG has mixed sentiment among the analyst community, and I tend to lean more neutral but with a bullish tilt, especially this early on in its public tenure. At the very least, I believe the stock can reclaim its IPO price, and I believe the results justify this move.

FIG Stock Metrics

Consensus EPS estimates for FIG currently are $0.22/share for 2026 and $0.52/share for fiscal 2029.

At both ends, the stock appears very richly valued. At today’s prices, FIG is over 100x its near-term earnings estimate and over 40x the longer-range estimate. I would find it hard to invest at these prices unless I had a high degree of confidence in the future outlook.

The broader Seeking Alpha (“SA”) community is similarly neutral. Wall Street analysts, however, are bullish, though coverage is relatively light. For those covering the stock, consensus price targets range from a low of $30/share to a high of nearly $100/share.

Can the stock reach these targets? Possibly over time, especially the low end of the range. There is little room for error with results, however, and that could create a steep mountain for FIG. That said, it appears FIG is outperforming on that front.

FIG Operating Performance

If there is one clear takeaway from the quarter, it’s that FIG is executing at a very high level.

Topline revenues in Q4, for example, were up 40% YOY and ahead of guidance. For the full year, revenue crossed the +$1B mark, growing 41%. Importantly, growth actually accelerated into Q4, which is not something you typically see from companies at this scale.

Retention and expansion metrics were equally impressive. Net dollar retention for customers spending more than +$10K in ARR jumped to 136%, up five points sequentially and the highest level in over two years.

In addition, gross retention held steady at 97%. This signals to me durability in the base customers. And among larger customers, the population continues to ramp meaningfully, with the +$100K+ ARR cohort growing 46% YOY and +$1M+ ARR customers up 68%.

Margins remain healthy as well. Gross margins held in the high-80% range despite surging AI usage, and the company delivered a 14% operating margin in Q4 with positive free cash flow. While investment is increasing, particularly in AI, the underlying business model looks strong, in my view.

Based on results, I believe the demand signals appear valid. The question now becomes how much of that strength is already priced into the stock at current trading levels.

What Is The Outlook For FIG Stock?

Looking ahead, management is clearly leaning into the momentum from their first half-year of being a public company.

For Q1 2026, FIG is guiding to revenue between +$315M and $317M. This would imply roughly 38% growth. For the full year, guidance calls for nearly +$1.4B in revenue, or about 30% growth at the midpoint. That’s a step down from 2025’s 41% pace, but still very strong by most standards.

What stands out to me is that the outlook assumes continued seat expansion, international growth, and increasing AI adoption. Roughly 75% of paid customers with more than +$10K in ARR are already consuming AI credits weekly, and beginning in March, FIG will monetize both seats and credits. That shift introduces upside potential but also some modeling uncertainty, in my view.

On a more negative note, margins are expected to compress, with full-year operating margin guided to around 8% as investment in AI accelerates. That tells me management is prioritizing long-term positioning over near-term profitability.

Analysts appear to be positive on the outlook but are more measured on valuation, with both RBC and J.P. Morgan maintaining neutral-equivalent ratings while trimming price targets amid multiple compression across software.

If FIG continues to execute and AI monetization ramps smoothly, the targets look achievable. But expectations remain high, and there is little room for missteps in this environment, in my view.

Is FIG Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

After walking through the numbers and the outlook, I find myself incrementally more bullish.

The valuation is not cheap by any traditional metric. Trading at over 100x near-term earnings and more than 40x longer-range estimates is a premium multiple, no doubt. But premium multiples are often reserved for premium growth, and right now, FIG is delivering.

Revenue growth accelerated to 40% in Q4. Net dollar retention hit 136%. Large customers are scaling. AI adoption is already embedded across the base, with monetization of credits just beginning. These are not signs of a company losing relevance in an AI-driven world; they are signs of one leaning into it, in my view.

Yes, operating margins are set to compress as investment ramps. Yes, expectations are high. And yes, there is little room for execution missteps. But the stock is down more than 30% YTD and trading below its IPO price of $33/share. Based on the current trajectory, I believe reclaiming that IPO level is reasonable and justified by the results and forward guidance.

For investors comfortable with volatility and long-term execution risk, I believe FIG is a worthy speculative flyer for those seeking outsized medium- to long-term gains.

data.title: The Cheesecake Factory: A Sweet Treat For Shareholders
data.description:
We view The Cheesecake Factory Incorporated (CAKE) stock as a Hold here following a nice rally from the fall of 2025 lows. The valuation and growth combination suggests the stock is about fairly valued for the level of growth. That said, as far as restaurants are concerned, The Cheesecake Factory is delivering stable growth, which is difficult in this competitive space. In this column, we discuss the just-reported quarter from our long-term house position in this stock. We particularly like that not only is there slow and stable growth, but there is a growing dividend and share repurchases.

The Cheesecake Factory Revenue Comparable Same Store Sales

Whenever we analyze the performance of a company in the restaurant space, comparable sales are the key metric we hone in on, followed by the overall growth in revenues. For Cheesecake Factory in Q4, revenues once again rose in Q4 vs. the prior year's Q4. The Cheesecake Factory delivered a top-line sales figure of $961.6 million, up 4.4% year-over-year. This beat consensus estimates by $12.7 million, as sales were expected to be closer to flat. However, that came with comparable sales results that were down 2.2% from last year's quarter. How did revenues rise if comps fell? Well, the company had about 7% unit growth from a year ago, so the revenue was influenced by more sales, including the seven new restaurants that opened up in Q4. How about margin power?

The Cheesecake Factory Margins

So first, food and beverage costs were 21.6% of sales, down from the 22.3% of last year, which was a 70-basis-point improvement. Further, labor expenses were just 33.9% of sales, which was down from 34.2% last year, an additional 40-basis-point improvement. We like this improvement as it translates to better margins. However, general and administrative expenses were 7.0% of overall revenues, rising from 6.3% last year, but other operating expenses and costs were 20 basis points lower than a year ago. But some lease expenses and preopening costs drove overall margin lower. There was a 160 basis point softening in operating margin to 3.5% from 5.1% last year.

CEO David Overton commented on the strong performance:

We delivered solid fourth quarter and full-year results in 2025, generating record annual revenue supported by 25 new restaurant openings for the year...despite a more challenging operating environment across the restaurant industry, including weather-related impacts, revenue for the quarter finished within our expected range. Margins and adjusted diluted net income per share finished toward the higher end of our expectations.

So a record quarter for revenues is always welcomed, and despite the overall operating margin decline, it was better than management had expected. The sales print, as we saw, was helped by more openings. The company plans to open up to 26 new restaurants in 2026 across its several banner names. Margin power is being well-controlled as far as labor and food costs are concerned, so we will have to see if the lease expense and opening-related expenses can be controlled and drive earnings even higher.

The Cheesecake Factory Earnings Power

The Q4 results showed declining comparable sales, margins that narrowed but accretive unit growth, and a better-than-expected overall top line. Putting together revenue and expenses, we saw Q4 adjusted EPS of $1.00, which was essentially in line (a one-penny beat). For the year, there was 10% EPS growth to $3.77 from $3.44. Assuming 8-10% EPS growth is possible for 2026, it would suggest that at today's share price of $65, shares are valued appropriately at 15-16X FWD EPS.

The Cheesecake Factory Raises The Dividend

Another reason we are happy to hold a house position is the growing dividend. In fact, the company just announced an 11.1% increase to the quarterly dividend, so it now pays $0.30 quarterly. While the yield is about 2%, which is not large by any stretch, over the long term, dividend growth investing is a winning play, something we love at BAD BEAT Investing. But it was the first raise in 15 quarters, so it would be better, of course, to get annual increases.

That said, along with the dividend hike, last week the board approved an increase of 5.0 million shares to the existing repurchase program, raising the total authorization to 66 million shares, which means about 6 million shares can be repurchased still. In Q4 another 228,100 shares were retired. So this boosts EPS power and shareholder value.

Forward View

We like holding here, and rate shares a market perform at these levels. New money could pounce if there is another wave lower in shares to the mid-$50s, as the risk-reward would be better there given the slow and stable growth. We think 8-10% EPS growth is possible on the high side, though consensus is for $4.03 in 2026, which is 6.9% growth. So overall, we think it is a Hold here, collect the dividend, and let the company slowly grow.
